passages.title: Pride and Prejudice
passages.attribution: Austen, Jane. Pride and Prejudice. T. Egerton, 1813. Project Gutenberg, ebook 1342, www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342. Chapter 1. British spellings (“neighbourhood”) follow the original.

tokens.craft: A dummy pronoun — it refers to nothing concrete, just holds the subject slot so Austen can delay her real claim until the end of the sentence. This is called an expletive construction, and it gives her total control over where the reader's attention lands.

A linking verb so small it almost disappears — yet it carries the whole satirical weight of the sentence. Austen mimics the style of philosophical proclamations ('It is a truth…') to deliver a petty social observation.
tokens.role: Main verb; links It to its predicate nominative.

tokens.craft: Indefinite article — one of the three articles (a, an, the), the most common adjectives. And a quietly devastating choice: she doesn't say the truth (which would be absolute); she says a truth, one among many, already undercutting the sentence's grand posture.
Abstract noun giving the sentence its mock-philosophical register. Austen is borrowing the vocabulary of Enlightenment reasoning to talk about husband-hunting.

An intensifier pushed to comic excess — as if this petty social observation held true in every society on earth. Its placement between truth and acknowledged makes it feel pompous, which is the point.

Past participle of acknowledge working as an adjective. It gives the sentence a stiff, formal quality — as though someone had already voted on this and declared it law. Austen is parodying exactly that kind of social decree.
tokens.role: Participle modifying truth from behind — a formal, Latinate position.

tokens.craft: Subordinating conjunction opening the noun clause that contains the actual content of the sentence. Everything before this word is prologue — the real claim begins here.
tokens.role: Introduces the noun clause that spells out what the truth is.

tokens.craft: Indefinite article again — a single man, not the single man. The observation applies to any and all such men, universally. (Note the irony: the article signals universality while also signaling ordinary typicality.)
The only quality that matters in this novel's marriage market. Not handsome, not young, not kind — just single. Austen reduces the entire man to his marital status.
A deliberately generic noun. Austen is not describing anyone specific — she is describing the category, the institution, the social fact.
tokens.role: Subject of must be inside the noun clause.

tokens.craft: Begins a prepositional phrase that defines the man's one relevant quality before we reach the punch line: he must have money.
tokens.role: Heads the phrase in possession of a good fortune, modifying man.

tokens.craft: A formal, almost legalistic word. To be 'in possession of' something sounds like an estate document. Keep this word in mind — Austen will flip it at the end of the next sentence.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase of a good fortune.

tokens.craft: Regency code for 'large.' A 'good fortune' doesn't mean a morally admirable one; it means a financially substantial one. Austen's adjectives often mean the opposite of what they seem to.
The real subject of the whole novel: money. Every comedy of manners that follows is built on this noun.
A modal verb expressing necessity — not probability, not likelihood, but must. The comedy turns on this word. He doesn't merely tend to want a wife; society has determined that he must.
Linking verb. Together with must, it states the condition as an axiom of nature.
tokens.role: Main verb of the noun clause.
Heads the phrase in want of a wife.

tokens.craft: Old-fashioned usage: 'want' here means lack or need, not desire. The irony is rich — the man himself may feel no such need, but society has assigned it to him anyway.

The punchline. Held for the very last word. Every piece of philosophical scaffolding — truth, universally, acknowledged, must — has been built to deposit us at this one ordinary noun. The anticlimax is the joke.
Here however doesn't mean 'but' — it means 'no matter how.' It launches a concession: however little may be known about the man himself, the neighborhood has already decided what he is for.
tokens.role: Modifies little, setting up the concessive opening.

tokens.craft: An adverb of degree, not an adjective — it tells how much the feelings are known (barely at all), rather than describing the feelings themselves. Austen pulls the whole phrase to the front of the sentence for emphasis.

Past participle in the passive verb may be known, yanked to the front of the sentence by the inversion. The man's inner life is grammatically acted upon — it doesn't get to act. That mirrors how the neighborhood treats him.
tokens.role: Main participle of the passive may be known; the inversion pulls it forward.

tokens.craft: Abstract noun — but notice it's the feelings of the man, not the women. Austen is already overturning the assumption that women are the emotional, irrational party in this arrangement.
Coordinating conjunction joining feelings and views — Austen offers both inner emotion and reasoned opinion, insisting the man is a full person even if nobody around him treats him as one.
A more intellectual word than feelings — his reasoned opinions, his perspective. The sentence insists the man has an inner life, even as the neighborhood ignores it.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase of such a man.

tokens.craft: A pointing adjective — referring back to the man described in sentence one. It ties the sentences together while keeping a satirical distance (such a man, as if he were a specimen).
Modal verb softer than must — possibility, not necessity. The man's own feelings get may; society's claim about him got must. That gap between the two modals contains the whole satirical argument.

tokens.role: Completes the passive may be known (whose participle was pulled to the front).
Heads the phrase on his first entering a neighbourhood — tells when.

tokens.craft: A possessive pronoun in front of an -ing word — the formal way to mark who is doing the entering when the -ing form is used as a noun. Austen's grammar is as polished as her irony.
Since entering is working as a noun here, first describes it the way an adjective describes any noun — the first entering, as opposed to later visits.
An -ing verb form working as a noun — a gerund. It names the event of arrival, and it still keeps a verb's appetite: it takes a neighbourhood as its own object. One word doing two kinds of work.
tokens.role: Gerund — object of the preposition on, taking a neighbourhood as its object.

tokens.craft: British spelling, straight from the 1813 text. The neighbourhood — not any named person — is the real actor in this sentence, which is exactly Austen's point.
Demonstrative adjective pointing straight back at the 'truth universally acknowledged.' The word keeps the mock-philosophical thread running through the paragraph.
The same noun as in sentence one, repeated like a term in a logical proof — except what follows shows that this 'truth' is really just what the neighbours have all agreed to want.

tokens.role: Helping verb of the passive is fixed.

tokens.craft: The first half of a so … that frame: the truth is so well fixed that he is considered property. Austen builds the neighborhood's certainty into the sentence's very skeleton.
Fixed well — thoroughly, securely. The adverb of a job professionally done, as if the community had installed this belief like a fence post.
Past participle in the passive is fixed. Nobody in particular did the fixing — the belief simply is fixed, the way passive verbs let communities dodge responsibility for their own opinions.
tokens.role: Main participle of the passive is (so well) fixed.
Heads the phrase in the minds… — tells where the truth is fixed.

tokens.craft: The truth lives nowhere in the world — only in the minds of the families. Austen locates her 'universal truth' precisely where it actually resides: inside the heads of interested parties.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase of the surrounding families.

tokens.craft: Present participle as adjective — and a mildly predatory one. The families don't just live nearby; they surround, the way a hunting party surrounds. One participle turns a village into an ambush.
The second half of the so … that frame — introducing the result of all that fixing.
tokens.role: Introduces the result clause paired with so.

tokens.craft: The man has dwindled to a pronoun — and a grammatically passive one at that. He performs no action in this entire sentence; things are merely settled about him.

tokens.role: Helping verb of the passive is considered.

tokens.craft: Passive again — considered by whom? By everyone and no one. Three passives in one sentence (known, fixed, considered) build a world where opinions have no owners.
tokens.role: Main participle of the passive is considered.
Links considered to what he is considered to be.

tokens.craft: The rightful property — definite article, as though his ownership were already a settled legal fact rather than a fantasy the families share.
A legal word — rightful is what you call an heir or an owner. The adjective smuggles courtroom certainty into what is actually pure presumption.

The trap snaps shut. Sentence one had the man 'in possession of a good fortune'; sentence two makes him the property. The possessor becomes the possessed — Austen's whole marriage-market critique in one flipped noun.
tokens.role: Object of as — what he is considered to be.
Heads the phrase of some one or other…

tokens.craft: Spelled as two words in 1813. And note how vague it is — some one or other. It doesn't matter which daughter gets him. Any will do. The indefiniteness is the insult.
The shrug completed: some one or other. The phrase waves a hand at the whole interchangeable set of daughters.
The final word of the paragraph, exactly as wife was the final word of the sentence before. Austen ends both sentences on the women the whole system is really about.

passages.title: A Farewell to Arms
passages.attribution: Hemingway, Ernest. A Farewell to Arms. Charles Scribner's Sons, 1929. Chapter 1. Published in 1929; in the United States public domain since 1 Jan. 2025.

tokens.craft: Hemingway opens with a preposition, not a subject — we land in the middle of a place before we meet any person. It's a cinematic technique: establish setting first.
tokens.role: Heads the phrase In the late summer of that year.

tokens.craft: Late summer, not deep summer or golden summer. Hemingway's adjectives are plain and precise — they tell you exactly what you need and nothing more.
A concrete, specific season — but note he doesn't name the year. That year is deliberately vague, creating a feeling of memory rather than reportage.
Demonstrative adjective pointing backward to a shared memory — as if reader and narrator already know which year. It creates false intimacy, the illusion of prior knowledge.
Hemingway's first-person plural — not I, but we. The narrator is immediately part of a collective, a unit, an army. This positioning quietly signals that the individual is subsumed by the group.
Simple past tense. Hemingway almost never reaches for the past perfect or present perfect — no had lived, no have lived. Just the clean, finished simplicity of lived. The past is over; here it is.
Not a villa, not a billet, not a lodge — just a house. Hemingway strips away the glamour of war and the romance of Italy by using the most ordinary noun available.
Again: not a town, not a city, not a hamlet. Village — small, modest, human-scaled. The scale keeps shrinking: country → village → house.
Relative pronoun introducing a descriptive clause — the village looked across a river and a plain. The landscape is opened out with this simple pronoun.
tokens.role: Subject of the relative clause that looked across…, standing for village.

tokens.craft: Personification verb — the village looks, like a person. Hemingway animates the landscape just enough to give it a perspective, then drops the technique.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase across the river and the plain.

tokens.craft: A landmark of Hemingway's landscape — rivers recur throughout his work as symbols of movement, time, and boundary. Here it's simply geographic, but it will gather meaning.
The plain will dominate the visual world of this novel — open, exposed, offering no shelter. Its introduction here is matter-of-fact, but it quietly establishes the vulnerability of the landscape.
tokens.role: Joined with river as second object of across.

tokens.craft: The third noun in the landscape series: river, plain, mountains. The vista is wide — and distant. The mountains will represent safety and escape throughout the novel; their appearance here sets that up before the reader knows to notice.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase In the bed of the river.

tokens.craft: The only domestic-sounding detail in an outdoor paragraph. Bed suggests rest and shelter — and Hemingway immediately fills it with dry stone, undercutting any comfort the word carries.
The river returns — but now it's described from the inside, its stony bed. The narrator is moving closer, zooming in.
Existential there — not really a place word here, but a structural word that lets Hemingway delay the true subject (pebbles and boulders) until after the verb. A subtle reversal of normal word order.
tokens.role: Expletive — fills the subject slot so the true subject can follow the verb.

tokens.craft: Simple past of be. Another stripped-down verb — no action, just existence. Things were. Hemingway trusts existence to be interesting enough.
Concrete, specific, physical. Hemingway is famous for making his readers see — not by describing impressions but by naming things exactly.
Escalation: pebbles, then boulders. The scale of the river bottom is enormous — this is not a gentle stream.
tokens.role: Joined with pebbles as second subject of were.

tokens.craft: One word that changes everything. Dry pebbles and boulders — the river is low or spent. The dryness signals the end of something, a season gone. In a war novel, this carries ominous weight.
tokens.role: Modifies pebbles and boulders from behind, with white.

tokens.craft: The stones' color is the absence of color — bleached by sun and water, empty of life. Hemingway's palette is already doing thematic work.
The sun appears for the first time — and it bleaches, it dries, it creates emptiness. Not warmth or joy. The natural world in this novel does not comfort.

Hemingway's favorite conjunction, joining two complete clauses. He strings observations together with and instead of ranking them with subordination — everything given equal weight, like items in an inventory.
tokens.role: Joins the two independent clauses of this compound sentence.

tokens.craft: The river returns as water — the only thing with energy in this static, bleached landscape.

tokens.role: Linking verb connecting water to its three descriptions.

tokens.craft: The water is transparent, readable, honest — qualities the war is not. The landscape's clarity sets up the fog of everything that follows.
One of the few adverbs in the passage — Hemingway restricts them severely. When one appears, it carries weight. Swiftly is the only urgency in this otherwise still world.
Present participle working as a predicate adjective, parallel with clear and blue. The only ongoing action in the passage — everything else is finished past tense; only the water keeps moving.
tokens.role: Participle as predicate adjective after was, parallel to clear and blue.

tokens.craft: The single warm note in the paragraph — and Hemingway confines it to the deep channels, where the water still runs. Life persists, but only in narrow grooves.
A precise geological term — the grooves carved by flowing water. Hemingway doesn't say 'streams' or 'currents'; he picks the exact technical word.

The first word that locates us in the war — and Hemingway gives it no introduction, no article (the troops or some troops), just the bare plural noun. Troops appears like a fact of nature, as inevitable as the river and the plain.
The simplest possible verb of motion. Not marched, not advanced, not tramped — just went. Hemingway drains the heroism from military movement with a single plain verb.
tokens.role: Main verb of the first clause.

tokens.craft: The house from the first sentence — now the troops pass it. The domestic space and the military space share the same geography, creating the novel's central tension.

tokens.role: Joins the two prepositional phrases by the house and down the road.

tokens.craft: Directional preposition giving the movement a vector — downward, toward the front, toward the war.
One of Hemingway's most loaded nouns. Roads in his fiction mean movement, fate, the direction history is forcing you. This road leads to the war.
The third and of the sentence — Hemingway chains clauses instead of subordinating them, and the flat, additive rhythm makes the war's traffic feel routine.
The first physical trace of the war's presence. It rises, it coats, it obscures. Dust will return as a motif throughout the novel.

The troops become they — a collective pronoun inside a compressed clause: the dust [that] they raised. Hemingway drops the relative pronoun entirely; the prose economizes just as the description does.
tokens.role: Subject of the tucked-in clause they raised, which describes dust.

tokens.craft: The verb of the tucked-in clause, not of the sentence. The troops act, but their only recorded product is dust — not victory, not heroism.
tokens.role: Verb of the clause they raised, modifying dust.

tokens.craft: The main verb of the clause — and a startlingly delicate one. Dust powders the leaves the way makeup powders a face. The gentleness of the verb makes the image more unsettling, not less.
tokens.role: Main verb — the dust … powdered the leaves.

tokens.craft: Suddenly we are aware the landscape is alive — it has vegetation, things that can be coated and choked. The war is doing this to the world.
Another simple, plain noun. The trees stand quietly while the troops and their dust pass — passive witnesses, like the narrator.

passages.title: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
passages.attribution: Douglass, Frederick. Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave. Anti-Slavery Office, 1845. Project Gutenberg, ebook 23, www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23. Chapter 1.

tokens.craft: The first word of the book is the self. For a man the law counted as property, opening with I — the grammar of a person, a subject who acts — is itself an argument.

tokens.role: Helping verb of the passive was born.

tokens.craft: The plainest of beginnings — the same verb every autobiography uses. Douglass claims the standard opening of a life story, insisting his life is a life story.

The first of five prepositions in a row that pin the birthplace down with almost legal precision: in… near… from… in… Douglass can document exactly where he was born. Watch what he cannot document in the next sentence.
A real, mappable place — a proper noun. Douglass stacks up verifiable geography because his whole narrative doubles as testimony: these things happened, and they happened here.
About twelve miles — approximately. Even the small hedge is precise: Douglass tells you exactly how sure he is of each fact.
An exact number in an exact record. The specificity is the style: this sentence reads like a deposition on purpose.

tokens.role: Noun of measure telling how far from Easton.
Heads the phrase in Talbot county, Maryland.

tokens.craft: A proper noun doing an adjective's job — which county? Talbot county. English lets nouns moonlight as modifiers, and formal prose leans on the move constantly.
tokens.role: Proper noun used as an adjective, modifying county.

tokens.craft: The widest ring of the map: town, neighborhood, distance, county, state. The sentence zooms out like a surveyor's report — and every ring is verifiable.
tokens.role: Appositive naming the state that contains Talbot county.

tokens.craft: After a sentence of total geographic certainty, the flat negative. Douglass knows exactly where — and is denied exactly when. The paragraph's whole design is that contrast.
He has some knowledge — rumor, guesswork — just nothing accurate. The adjective is scrupulously fair, which makes the accusation underneath it more damning, not less.
An abstract noun for the thing slavery denied him. The ledger-keeping masters recorded the ages of livestock; a person's birthday went unrecorded on purpose.
The most basic fact a person can own about themselves — and he doesn't own it. One plain noun carries the deprivation.
An absolute — not rarely, not seldom. Never. The adverb closes every loophole before a skeptical reader can open one.

A perfect participle (having seen) heading the phrase that explains the missing knowledge. Douglass packs the reason into a participial phrase instead of spending a whole new sentence on it — compression a lawyer would admire.
tokens.role: Heads the participial phrase never having seen…, which modifies I and explains why.

tokens.craft: Not the record, not a record — any authentic record. The word sweeps the whole category empty.
Documents run through this whole opening: records, knowledge, testimony. Douglass writes like a man building a case file, because he is.
A second participle nested inside the first phrase — record containing it. Which record? One containing his age. The participle does an adjective clause's job (that contained it) in a single word.
tokens.role: Participle modifying record, taking it as its object.

tokens.craft: By far — an idiom of degree. Idioms like this resist tidy parsing: the two words work as a single unit meaning 'to the greatest extent,' boosting the larger part.
tokens.role: Heads the idiom by far, which strengthens the larger part.

tokens.craft: A comparative adjective used with measured restraint — the larger part, not 'almost all' or 'every one.' Douglass never claims an inch more than he can defend.
The verb of the sentence is know — this whole opening is about knowledge: who is permitted to have it and who is kept from it.
The first half of the comparison frame as little … as. Douglass is about to make the frame do terrible work.
tokens.role: Adverb of degree modifying little; pairs with the second as.

tokens.craft: Here little works as a noun — a small amount, the thing known. 'Know as little of their ages as…' The word names the near-nothing they are allowed to know.

The second as — a subordinating conjunction opening the comparison clause. And the comparison is the sentence's knife: as horses know of theirs. Douglass makes the reader perform the equation slavery performed — people counted like livestock — and feel its obscenity.
tokens.role: Introduces the comparison clause as horses know of theirs.

tokens.craft: The most brutal noun in the passage, delivered without raising his voice. The analogy is not Douglass's invention — it is slavery's own bookkeeping, quoted back at it.
A possessive pronoun standing alone — theirs = their ages. The parallel grammar (their ages… theirs) locks people and horses into the same sentence pattern, which is exactly the horror.

tokens.role: Joins the two independent clauses of this compound sentence.

tokens.craft: An anticipatory it, holding the subject seat while the real subject — to keep their slaves thus ignorant — waits at the end of the sentence. The delay lets the sentence land on the word ignorant.
tokens.role: Placeholder subject; the true subject is the infinitive phrase at the end.

tokens.craft: Not the practice or the habit — the wish. Ignorance isn't an accident of the system; it is desired, intended, chosen. One noun assigns the motive.
tokens.role: Predicate nominative renaming the delayed subject.

tokens.craft: Again the careful measurement: most masters, not all. Douglass's precision about scope is what makes him impossible to dismiss.

Within my knowledge — the witness's oath again. He testifies only to what he has seen. The phrase also quietly asserts that he, the 'ignorant' one, is the person in this sentence who actually knows things.
Not a preposition here — this to is the sign of an infinitive (to keep). The infinitive phrase is the sentence's true subject, held back until the end.
Keep — maintain, hold in place, enforce. Ignorance requires upkeep, like a fence. The verb makes the masters' effort visible.
tokens.role: Infinitive; with its objects it forms the true subject of is.

tokens.craft: A cool, formal adverb — thus, in this manner, as just described. The Latinate register keeps the sentence's temperature low while its content burns.

The final word of the paragraph. Keep their slaves ignorant — the adjective completes the object, describing the state the masters enforce. Douglass ends on the word so the reader cannot look away from it.

passages.title: To Build a Fire
passages.attribution: London, Jack. “To Build a Fire.” 1908. Lost Face, Macmillan, 1910. Project Gutenberg, ebook 2429, www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2429. London's British spellings (“grey,” “little-travelled”) follow that edition.

tokens.craft: London opens with a bare, unmodified noun — no article, no adjective. Just Day in its elemental form, like a force of nature. The absence of the or a makes it feel mythic rather than specific.
Auxiliary forming the past perfect — an action completed before the story's present moment. The day has already broken; we are arriving after the beginning.
tokens.role: Helping verb forming the past perfect with broken.

tokens.craft: Past participle. London uses break instead of dawn or come — days don't gently arrive in this story, they break, suggesting violence, rupture, sudden exposure.
tokens.role: Main participle of the past perfect had broken.

tokens.craft: The single most important adjective in the story. Temperature is the antagonist; everything follows from this word. London introduces it immediately, with no build-up.
tokens.role: Predicate adjective describing the day after had broken.

tokens.craft: Paired with cold, establishing a monochrome, lifeless world. (London's British spelling, kept from the 1910 text.) The palette has already been drained of color and warmth — we know this before we know anything else.

An intensifier of near-comic formality — as if the narrator cannot help himself, cannot stop emphasizing just how cold it is. In Hemingway's spare prose this word would be cut; London keeps it because his narrator is not restrained.
Repeated. London says it twice in the same sentence. He doesn't trust a single mention to convey what he needs you to feel. The repetition hammers.
tokens.role: Second instance — reinforces the already-stated quality.

tokens.craft: Also repeated. Four adjectives, two words, total. The sentence's vocabulary has been reduced to match the world it describes.
Subordinating conjunction marking the moment of the man's fatal decision. Everything in this sentence has been setting up for this when.
tokens.role: Introduces the adverb clause telling when the day broke.

tokens.craft: The man — definite article, no name. London refuses to individualize him; he is every overconfident human who has ever underestimated nature. The article makes him archetypal.
Not Tom, not Jack, not any name. Just the man — the story's only human, and he stays generic to the end.
A quiet, unheroic verb for a fatal decision. He doesn't veer or plunge — he simply turned aside. The ordinariness of the verb makes the tragedy more terrible.
tokens.role: First verb of the adverb clause.

tokens.craft: One small directional word for the choice that kills him. He leaves the trail — this is all London needs to say. Aside is the entire plot in five letters.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase from the main Yukon trail.

tokens.craft: Main — the safe, traveled, established route. London uses the plainest possible word and lets context give it enormous weight. The main trail is survival; leaving it is death.
A proper noun doing an adjective's job — which trail? The Yukon trail. It places the scene in the harshest inhabited territory in North America; readers in 1908 knew exactly what the Yukon meant.
tokens.role: Proper noun used as an adjective, modifying trail.

tokens.craft: A line of safety through trackless wilderness. The whole plot turns on his leaving it — London establishes what it is before telling us he left it.

tokens.role: Joins the clause's two verbs, turned and climbed.

tokens.craft: The second verb the man performs — physical effort, upward motion. He works against the landscape from his first appearance.
tokens.role: Second verb of the adverb clause; shares the subject man.

tokens.craft: A hyphenated compound noun, plain as dirt — because it is dirt. London builds his Yukon out of blunt physical nouns, not scenery-painting.

A relative adverb opening a clause that describes the bank — where a dim trail led eastward. The same word students meet in adverb clauses is doing adjective-clause work here; the job, not the word, decides.
The other trail is dim — faint, barely marked, barely used. One syllable of warning that the man ignores.
A compound participle: little + travelled fused with a hyphen into one modifier. Few have come this way — and the word itself records their absence.
Trails lead — the verb quietly gives the path agency, as if it, not the man, decides where this story goes. In this landscape, that is nearly true.
A compass-point adverb, precise as a map. London's narrator always knows exactly where things are; only the man is lost.
tokens.role: Modifies led — tells where the trail led.
Heads the phrase through the fat spruce timberland.

tokens.craft: A strange, wonderful adjective for forest — fat spruce timberland, dense and thick-grown. London grabs a word from the body and presses it onto the land.
A noun (the tree) working as an adjective — what kind of timberland? Spruce timberland. Stacked modifiers, each one concrete.
tokens.role: Noun used as an adjective, modifying timberland.

tokens.craft: A dummy it — the same expletive move as Austen's opening, but doing plain work instead of satire: 'It was a steep bank' simply gets the description started.
The man's first weakness: he paused for breath. The body is already objecting to this journey, and the story has barely begun.
tokens.role: Main verb of the second clause.
Heads the phrase for breath — tells why he paused.

tokens.craft: A participial phrase — excusing the act to himself — hung onto the sentence's end. The man lies to himself in a modifier: he pretends he stopped to check the time, not to rest. London diagnoses the character's pride in a phrase most readers coast right over.
Reflexive pronoun — the excuse has an audience of one. Nobody else is out here to fool, which is precisely the man's problem.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase by looking at his watch — tells how he excused it.

tokens.craft: An -ing form as a noun — a gerund, the object of by. How did he excuse the pause? By looking. The gerund names the little performance he puts on for himself.
tokens.role: Gerund — object of by, taking the phrase at his watch.

tokens.craft: The watch — man's portable, mechanical time — carried into a landscape that runs on a different clock entirely. The next sentence reads the watch; the story will show how little it matters.
Another dummy it — the standard way English tells the time ('It was nine o'clock'). No suspense, no delay: just the fact, stamped like an entry in a log.
tokens.role: Placeholder subject for the time statement.

tokens.craft: A precise number — not nearly ten or past eight. This specificity of time, against the vagueness of who the man is, creates an eerie documentary effect.
A time-of-day marker. Morning. The man has a full day ahead — and squanders all of it.
Existential There — an expletive that delays the real subject. Compare Hemingway's 'there were pebbles and boulders': both writers use the same construction to lay out a landscape.
tokens.role: Expletive — fills the subject slot; the true subject is sun.

tokens.craft: Absolute negative adjective — not little sun or weak sun but no sun. The sun has been eliminated from this world entirely.
The absent sun. Without it there is no warmth, no reference point, no orientation. The man is in a world that has removed its primary life-giving feature.
A negative conjunction that escalates the absence: not just no sun, but no hint of sun. The negation is total.
tokens.role: Joins sun and hint of sun in the negative.

tokens.craft: A word for the smallest possible suggestion. Even the faintest, most indirect signal of the sun is absent. London removes warmth categorically.
A concession clause — though there was not a cloud in the sky. A cloudless sky with no sun: London builds the paradox into the grammar and lets the reader feel that something is deeply wrong.

tokens.role: Expletive again — the third existential construction of the paragraph.
Placeholder subject — 'It was a clear day.'

tokens.craft: Clear — and yet dark. The adjective sets up the sentence's pivot: every plain fact about this day contradicts the feel of it.
And yet — two coordinating conjunctions stacked, the second one turning against the first. The doubled hinge is where the sentence swings from fact to dread.
tokens.role: Marks the contrast between the clear day and the pall.

tokens.craft: Not was — seemed. The pall can't be measured or photographed; it exists in perception. London's one concession to the unmeasurable, and he flags it honestly with the verb.
tokens.role: Main verb (existential, with seemed in place of was).

tokens.craft: An before a vowel sound — the indefinite article's other form. Tiny word, automatic choice, but even it follows a rule about sound, not spelling.
A pall you cannot touch — intangible. The adjective concedes the thing isn't physical while insisting it is real. That tension powers the whole sentence.
A pall is the cloth draped over a coffin. London's word choice is not subtle: the sky wears a funeral shroud. The whole story's ending is folded into this noun, ninety seconds after the story begins.

tokens.role: Heads the phrase over the face of things.

tokens.craft: The world has a face — the metaphor personifies the landscape just enough for it to seem shrouded, like a body.
The vaguest noun in English, used deliberately: the gloom lies over everything, unnameable and total.
An appositive — a subtle gloom renames the intangible pall, the sentence circling its object, trying a second name for a thing that resists naming.

tokens.role: Relative pronoun; subject of made, standing for gloom.

tokens.craft: Made the day dark — the verb hands the gloom real power over the world. What merely seemed two clauses ago now makes things happen.
tokens.role: Verb of the relative clause; takes day and the complement dark.

tokens.craft: An object complement — the gloom made the day dark. The adjective completes the verb's work, describing what the day becomes.

tokens.role: Joins the two relative clauses about the gloom.
Verb of the second relative clause.

tokens.craft: After all the shrouds and gloom, the narrator turns coroner: the darkness was due to the absence of sun. Cause identified, mystery dissected — London's narrator explains everything except how to survive it.
tokens.role: Predicate adjective; due to points to the cause.
Completes due to; heads the phrase naming the cause.

tokens.craft: The paragraph's last idea is an absence — an abstract noun for a missing thing. The world of this story is defined by what it lacks: sun, warmth, names, company.
The third time sun appears in the paragraph, each time as something missing. Repetition as drumbeat: no sun, no hint of sun, absence of sun.

passages.title: “Because I could not stop for Death”
passages.meta: Emily Dickinson, written c. 1863 — stanzas 1 & 2, as first published in 1890
passages.attribution: Dickinson, Emily. “The Chariot” [“Because I could not stop for Death”]. c. 1863. Poems, edited by Mabel Loomis Todd and T. W. Higginson, Roberts Brothers, 1890, p. 138. This is the public-domain first-edition text; Dickinson's manuscript uses dashes and extra capitals (“the Carriage,” “His Civility”) that her first editors smoothed away.

tokens.craft: Dickinson opens the poem with a subordinating conjunction — the whole first line is a dependent clause. She delays her main point (Death kindly stopped) until after she explains why. The effect is intimate, even apologetic: she couldn't stop; she was busy.
tokens.role: Opens the adverb clause; the main clause waits in line 2.

tokens.craft: First-person — the speaker is the whole poem's center. But this is a posthumous speaker, which Dickinson doesn't tell us yet. The I is ordinary, almost casual.
Modal verb of ability — she could not, not would not. This is key: she didn't refuse Death; she simply didn't have time. The busyness of life is her only explanation.
The negation — she was unable to stop. Life kept her moving. Dickinson's speaker is not afraid of Death; she simply hasn't had a moment.
The simplest verb for ceasing motion. In context, stopping means dying — accepting death, turning toward it. She couldn't do it.
tokens.role: Main verb of the dependent clause.

tokens.craft: Stopping for someone means making time for them. Death, in this poem, is a figure one makes appointments with.
The poem's central noun — capitalized, personified, present as a character. Dickinson treats Death not as an event but as a gentleman caller who comes to escort her.
Death becomes He — a pronoun. The abstraction has been fully personified: a gentleman, a suitor, a driver. This pronoun makes Death intimate and oddly domestic.
tokens.role: Subject of stopped in the main clause.

tokens.craft: The poem's most startling adverb. Death kindly stopped — not grimly, not terrifyingly, but with gentleness and consideration. Dickinson's Death is polite. The strangeness of this one word is the poem's central trick.
Death did what the speaker could not: he stopped. He paused his eternal motion to collect her. The verb echoes stop from the first line — she couldn't; he did.
tokens.role: Main verb of the main clause.

tokens.craft: Object pronoun — Death stopped for me, specifically, personally. The intimacy of being chosen is slightly alarming. Death selected this speaker above all others.

The vehicle of death, the conveyance. In Dickinson's era carriage rides were courtship rituals; she casts dying as a social outing. (In her manuscript she capitalized it — the Carriage — one of many capitals her 1890 editors quietly removed. Compare the two and you can watch editing change a poem's temperature.)
A verb of containment — the carriage holds its passengers. But it also echoes the grip of something inescapable: once you're in, it holds you.
Here but isn't joining anything — it means only. 'Held but just ourselves': held only us. When but modifies like this, it's working as an adverb, not a conjunction. The job decides.
Doubled with but — 'but just ourselves' — the two limiting adverbs squeeze the guest list to its minimum. The carriage is intimate, not crowded. Death has arranged a private conveyance.
The speaker refers to herself and her companion as a unit — ourselves, almost a family already. She and Death share a pronoun before they've gone a mile.
A connector running across the line break — the third passenger arrives a line late, like a surprise. The enjambment makes you meet Immortality alone, on its own line.

The third passenger — capitalized, personified, even in the edited 1890 text. Immortality is not an idea here but a traveling companion. Its presence is paradoxical: the vehicle of death also carries eternal life.
tokens.role: Second object of held, joined by And across the line break.

tokens.craft: The first-person plural — the speaker has joined Death as we. She is no longer separate from these figures; she is traveling with them.
Death is not rushing. The carriage moves slowly — giving the speaker time to look out, to observe, to remember. The poem is an elegy for life, and life needs to be seen clearly before it ends.
Past tense — they drove. This is memory, a recollection of the journey toward death. Drove is plain and kinetic; the verb keeps the carriage moving through the poem.
Death knew — he has knowledge the speaker lacks. He is in control of the pace and route, a driver with a destination in mind.
tokens.role: Main verb of the second clause.

tokens.craft: Negative adjective — absolute. Death doesn't feel haste; he doesn't have a concept of it. He is beyond urgency.
Abstract noun — the condition of urgency, rush, hurry. Death has none of it. He is patient, untroubled, permanent. The noun gives the concept a physical weight.

tokens.role: Joins the clauses across the line break.

tokens.craft: Past perfect — the speaker had put away before the ride, at the moment she stepped into the carriage. The tense marks the exact instant her life's business ended.
A plain, domestic verb — you put away tools at the end of a workday. She surrenders labor and leisure with the same mundane gesture you'd use to close a cabinet.
tokens.role: Main verb of the past perfect.

tokens.craft: Completes the phrasal verb put away — to set aside, to surrender. The act of dying is recast as quiet, voluntary tidying-up.
The things she puts away belong to her — my labor, my leisure. They were hers; she yields them.
Her work, her effort, her living activity — one of the two things she must set aside to ride with Death. Labor is life; she is giving it up.
The companion to labor — not just work but rest, pleasure, free time. Everything. She gives up not only life's productive parts but its enjoyments. The carriage takes all of it.
A small word easy to skip — my leisure too. It adds leisure to the pile almost as an afterthought, the way one remembers one last thing to pack. The casualness is heartbreaking.
An exchange is being made — labor and leisure traded for his civility. The preposition prices the transaction: everything she had, for good manners.
tokens.role: Heads the phrase For his civility — the reason for the surrender.

tokens.craft: Death's civility belongs to him — a personal attribute, like a well-cut coat. (Dickinson's manuscript reads His Civility, capitals and all; the 1890 editors dressed it down.)

Death's defining characteristic in this poem — not terror, not darkness, but civility. A social virtue. This is the poem's central irony: death as good manners, dying as accepting a courteous invitation.
